docs.text: Lesson-writing Guidance and Conventions The purpose of this guide is to lay out format and style conventions for writing on Brilliant. The lessons we design are the bones of what we do, but consistent and clear language and format contribute to their clarity and sparkle. The draw of Brilliant is our ideas. Our medium, whether writing or images or interactives, can help deliver these ideas, or get in the way. Our best writing is direct and easy to read. The principles listed below reflect how formatting and language should support the ideas in our lessons. The more specific guidance in the four thematic sections are considered to be logical conclusions from these principles. :::caution This is a non-exhaustive reference. It is a living document that is updated regularly. If the guidance below doesn't address your specific problem, asking in the Slack channel #editorial-lounge is also encouraged.** ::: Composition Principles Reading flow — Layout of questions, text, images and interactives supports and complements a smooth reading experience. Cadence and timing — Each step is a single, complete thought. Each block is a complete achievement. Questions roles — Lightweight questions are prospective, setting up lesson concepts and intuition. MCQs ask learners to apply ideas introduced explicitly in a lesson. Information field — The learner's decisions should be as simple as possible. They should never be guessing about what to do. Minimize disruption — Screen clears should happen at expected junctures. Glossary cards and asides are meant to reduce friction, not momentum. Formatting Principles Sentences flow from beginning to end. We never write sentences that double back, or qualify statements while in the process of making them. Punctuation sets the cadence of our writing and delineates ambiguous situations. We should do this as visually as we can. Text formatting and layout draw attention to what's important. Emphasis everywhere is emphasis nowhere. Our language is accurate and precise, but never pedantic. Numbers, equations , and pseudocode are main players. We want them to have a tactile quality, like they're natural objects for the learner to manipulate. Sentences and Punctuation The purpose of a sentence is to advance a complete thought. There's no magic point where a sentence needs to end, but the best sentences are direct and purposeful. Punctuation is a tool to clarify the meaning of a sentence with pauses and separation. It can also be used to suggest a rhythm, but this is strictly less important than clarity. . Text Formatting and Layout The purpose of formatting is to promote reading comprehension. We make intentional use of whitespace and group sentences that form a step. The learner shouldn't have to perform a close reading to figure out the structure of our arguments in their head, our layout should show it. Key points that would otherwise get buried in a wall of text can be surfaced with boldface type, bulleted lists, line breaks, and the like. Use emphasis sparingly — emphasis everywhere is emphasis nowhere. Language At Brilliant, our business is teaching people how to think about complex things. The narrator is the learner's trusted guide in their quest for understanding, and they rely on us for the clarity and quality of our explanations. The learner is an active participant in the lesson and we write in the spirit of collaboration. The words we choose should make it as easy as possible to understand our ideas. Learners shouldn't have to spend time to pick apart our meaning — they need that time for the concepts. Our language is vivid and precise, leaving no question about the claims we make or when they apply. Formatting Numbers, Math, and Code On Brilliant, numbers, equations, and pseudocode are main players. We want them to have a tactile feel, like they're natural objects for the learner to play with and manipulate. To promote this, numbers, equations, and pseudocode are always formatted — without exception. The format signals to the learner "here are the things you're working with". Numbers, equations, and pseudocode are the language we use to convey precise meaning. They can appear inline with the written word or set apart, if they require closer inspection. Our symbolic conventions promote legibility and understanding. Numbers, equations, and pseudocode should not be disruptive in form or appearance. This means we want to be as consistent as possible across all courses. If you need to refer to a sophisticated expression many times, you should establish a name or symbol for it using a centered environment and use that for repeated inline reference.
docs.title: Lesson-writing Guidance and Conventions

docs.text: Adverbs and Adjectives The modern world has dissolved common meaning from qualitative adjectives and adverbs. Words like "quickly", "big", or "loud" don't have well understood meanings without familiarity with the speaker. This makes them poorly suited for Brilliant where we need to be precise and accurate. Avoid using qualitative adverbs for anything but relative comparisons. Absolute statements should never be made using a qualitative adverb or adjective. 🚨 The particle moves very quickly through the synchrotron. ✅ Protons are heavier than electrons.
docs.title: Adverbs and Adjectives

docs.text: Blue-Collar Writing 📏 Use the simplest possible words to express an idea — but no simpler. A Brilliant lesson is not an academic paper or textbook. Like a good newspaper, our writing informs a thoughtful, diverse, and interested audience. The words we choose make our ideas precise and memorable for this audience. If language gets in the way because it is ambiguous, or unfamiliar, or distracting, then it is not serving our purposes. We use a simple vocabulary and direct sentences to express ideas. We call this blue-collar writing because our audience is wider than the audience of STEM textbooks. Our words are almost always commonplace, but never boring. Use $5 words only if you need the specificity — if a more common word can achieve the same meaning, use the common one. Some words never pass this test, like "utilize". 🚨 When evaluating an expression with a repeated variable, we replace every occurrence of the variable with its specified value. ✅ Evaluating an expression means we replace every occurrence of the variable with its value. In particular, language used in storytelling should be as direct as possible. 🚨 Frustrated with your inability to resolve the disputes with your friend, you return to playing with your Decision Box. ✅ Frustrated by the disagreements, you return to playing with your Decision Box. Avoid "math speak." 🚨 Given that each right triangle below is isosceles (the legs in each triangle have the same length), what is x? ✅ Each right triangle below is isosceles — the legs in each triangle have the same length. What is $x$? Avoid colloquialisms. 🚨 This is all well and good when there's no acceleration, but what do we do when there is? ✅ This is fine when there's no acceleration, but what's different when there is? Choose simple words that don't have multiple meanings. 🚨 Determining whether a sum converges or diverges is a subtle business, and a large chunk of calculus is devoted to finding convergence tests. ✅ It can be tricky to determine whether a sum converges or diverges. Convergence testing is a large portion of a calculus course. Be intentional about your word choices. Avoid repeating high-entropy words in nearby sentences (or the same sentence). 🚨 Their shared love of ice climbing brought them back **together** after years apart. Once they were **together**, they'd never be apart. ✅ Their shared love of ice climbing brought them back **together** after years apart. From then on, they'd never be apart. Brilliant writers and Brilliant users hail from different countries, but we need to use a single English dialect in our courses. 📏 For consistency, Brilliant lessons use the American spellings of words.
docs.title: Blue-Collar Writing

docs.text: Bookends 📏 Bookends begin and end every lesson on Brilliant. They preview and summarize the lesson's purpose in non-technical and succinct language. They are never more than a few sentences. Bookends are signposts that orient the learner between lessons. They do not contain a full picture of the lesson. Rather, they highlight the essentials and invite the learner to keep going. The focus of the opening bookend is telling the learner what they will do in the current lesson. The opening bookend can mention content in earlier lessons, but this is not its main purpose. The closing bookend focuses on highlighting what the learner did in the lesson, mirroring the language of the opening bookend. If there's room, the closing bookend can look ahead to the next lessons to compel the learner to move forward. Example of an opening bookend Example of a closing bookend

docs.text: Interactive Solvables 📏 **Interactive Solvable** — The question setup text lives outside the solvable box. Only the interactive objective lives inside the box. The objective is stated inside the solvable box. If there is setup text, it appears above the solvable box. If an image accompanies the interactive solvable, then the image should be in the left lane and the interactive solvable on the right. Otherwise, images should be placed above the solvable box. Don't remind learners how the UI works. It is the job of the UI to make itself clear, and the writer's role to use it consistently, so learners know what to expect. Don't remind people about interactive features defensively. If something turns out to be a problem in playtesting, we can handle that in the interactive itself. Example Examples 🚫 This late in the course (chapter 5) we don't need the "Note:" below.
docs.title: Interactive Solvables

docs.text: Jargon and Technical Vocabulary 📏 Be intentional about the technical words you introduce. Avoid jargon that forms a barrier to understanding. 📏 Make consistent reference to technical concepts. Learners should emerge from a course on Brilliant able to speak knowledgeably on a subject. However, concepts are more important than technical words. The purpose of attaching technical name to important ideas and processes is to ease referencing them. Once you establish a technical name, use a glossary card to reinforce it, and use the term consistently. Sometimes, blocks of text can be shortened considerably by making a definition, even if the definition is not a global concept within the course. Definitions take up RAM in the learner's brain, but if making a definition streamlines the text or the statement of a question considerably, it's worth it. 🚨 The probability of drawing exactly one red ball on two attempts is the probability of picking a red ball on the first attempt and picking a blue ball on the second, plus the probability of picking a blue ball on the first attempt and a red ball on the second. ✅ The probability of drawing exactly one red ball on two attempts is $P(\text{pick 1 is red})P(\text{pick 2 is blue})$, plus $P(\text{pick 1 is blue})P(\text{pick 2 is red})$. Technical names should be reserved for only the most important concepts, not details that arise rarely. Someone can memorize the names and characteristics of 500 birds without understanding how they fly or how their metabolism works. Jargon-y text is the result of attaching names to ideas that are not central. Jargon can make a simple and important idea impenetrable. Do not introduce jargon in a Brilliant lesson.
docs.title: Jargon and Technical Vocabulary

docs.text: Lightweight Questions 📏 **Lightweight Questions** — Lightweight questions are used for when the user doesn't yet have the tools to know the right answer or when we want them to guess. Lightweight questions should be used when there is no right answer at the current level of understanding, a true right answer can't be justified the learner is supposed to take a guess (or something more playful) Don't use them when Envelope or another interactive solvable could be used instead, or The learner has all the information they need to answer, and there is a single right answer — this is the canonical case for MCQs Examples ✅ We don't expect the learner to be able to show that 2 is right, but it's useful to get them thinking about it. 🚫 We expect the learner to be able to solve this. Use an MCQ. Don't say "take a guess, don't worry" when a lightweight choice appears. Instead, consistently use lightweight choice and full MCQ for separate purposes. Examples 🚫 Here, it is clear from the use of lightweight choice that this is non-committal. Here are some wording suggestions: Lightweight — a definite answer can't be established yet What kind of relationship might there be between M and x? What do you think the relationship is between M and x? What is a plausible relationship between M and x? What do you expect the relationship between M and x could be? What do you suspect the relationship between M and x could be? MCQ — has a definite answer What is the relationship between M and x? How are M and x related? Don't do Guess the relationship between M and x. They appear in a single lane, unless multiple questions refer to an image or interactive in the right lane. A lightweight question should have a succinct question statement. The question refers to an image that is directly above it. Setup text is placed above the image. This is a proper use of a lightweight question: the question is not expecting the learner to use knowledge from the lesson to answer. Lightweight questions do not have official solutions, but text after the question should say whether the learner is right or wrong, or state that the correct answer will be revealed later in the lesson. Example

docs.text: MCQs 📏 When the question is an MCQ, the question setup text lives outside the solvable box. Only the question statement lives inside the box. The setup and motivation for the question is outside the solvable box. Only the question statement is inside the box. If a question in your lesson requires more details or assumptions, these should be woven into the preceding text. Hints shouldn't be stated in the text. Instead, use the ? hint command in Camperdown Example 📏 For MCQs that reference an image, the reference image or interactive lives outside the solvable box. If the displaying the image and question doesn't put the image off screen, a single lane is fine. Single-lane example If the image/interactive would run off the screen in single-lane, then move to double-lane format with the solvable in the right lane — unless multiple questions refer to the same image or interactive (see splitlanes guidance ). Double-lane example

docs.text: Modularity 📏 Ideas in a lesson are presented in a linear stream of thought. A user should be able to glance back through a block and easily identify its key points and arguments. In a textbook, paragraphs advance ideas. In a Brilliant lesson, we develop ideas in much smaller chunks. A Brilliant lesson is a conversation in which the learner participates by answering questions, manipulating interactives, and solving problems. The key to good conversation is balance among the participants. Saying too much or too little on your turn is a conversation killer. The aim is to say just enough to move the conversation forward. To begin writing the lesson, it may help to envision an actual conversation with someone who is learning the subject. What questions would they ask? How would you know they are understanding? Once you chart out your main points, group sentences according to which thoughts are best digested together. Deliver one idea at a time. Give the learner frequent opportunities to demonstrate that they follow. Keep in mind the block structure of lessons makes it difficult for learners to reference variables in previous blocks. If you are carrying over ideas from a previous block, it is helpful to have something to reference in the current block (like context you provide at the beginning of the block).
docs.title: Modularity

docs.text: Self-location Lessons late in the course should be written as if the learner has done what comes before. If the learner does not understand established concepts, we should make sure to supply more support, in the form of practice problems or extra lessons. But we should not write lessons as if the learner will enter the course in the middle. Build on the pedagogy of previous lessons — don't try to pioneer new analogies or frameworks for things that can be handled with what's been laid out. Make sure to return to the conceptual outline and point out problems or things that are missing. If you write a lesson based on something that's ambiguous in the outline, make sure to include your new assumptions at the top of the lesson sketch. Some of the lessons sketches string together substantial intermediate results that get used later, but the lesson is written as if I can see the entirety of the lesson. Information that's required for the question at hand should not require scrolling further back than the previous problem. If you need to reference information that isn't a current object of discussion — re-contextualize it before diving in. If you're invoking a formula that isn't already a current object of discussion, then recall it in your writing. Examples: ✅ Referencing earlier results (500 + 300t etc) 🚫 Include the numbers that I need to answer the question: fixed cost cost per team 🚫 Don't ask the learner to go back and look. If you need to, present that information above the solvable box.
docs.title: Self-location

Examples: ✅ Referencing earlier results (500 + 300t etc) 🚫 Include the numbers that I need to answer the question: fixed cost cost per team 🚫 Don't ask the learner to go back and look. If you need to, present that information above the solvable box.

Sentence Linearity 📏 Sentences should advance a single thought, flowing from beginning to end. The purpose of a sentence is to advance a complete thought. There's no magic point where a sentence needs to end, but the best sentences are direct and purposeful. Avoid making qualifying statements in the middle of making a point. If this seems necessary, consider whether you've clearly established your assumptions. 🚨 The angles of a triangle, except for those on a curved manifold, sum to 180 degrees. ✅ The angles of a triangle sum to 180 degrees. Establish the point early in each sentence. There are many silly ways that have been invented to start sentences with needless suspense. Outside the beginning of fairy tales, these are no good. They make the reader wonder what the sentence is going to be about as they read it. 🚨 Though it may be surprising, given how many sources of friction there are in real world systems, the sum of kinetic and potential energy is conserved. ✅ The sum of kinetic and potential energy is conserved, despite the many sources of friction in the real world.

docs.text: Sentence Smithing 📏 Chisel your sentences while preserving meaning The first draft of a sentence is usually not the one you want to publish. There are bound to be extra words, or complex structure that can be edited out. Don't wait around waiting for the most concise or elegant phrasing as you draft — write, slash, and wordsmith. Once you're sure about what you want your sentence to communicate, look over what you've written. Does anything fails to contribute to that meaning? If so, it can be deleted. The usual suspects include adverbs, multi-word phrases, extraneous details, phrases that are separated from the main clause by a comma. Chisel until you can't remove any more without changing its meaning.

docs.text: Solutions 📏 If a solution requires several images, or breaks naturally into a linear set of steps, use a stepped solution. Otherwise, it's usually okay to put the solution on a single step. Single-step format is good for short solutions, solutions that don't require a lot of images, and solutions that feature a stepped diagrammar. Avoid putting stepped diagrammars in a stepped solution, since the steppers for diagrammar and solutions look similar. If you have more than one independent visual for a solution, then don't use a single-step solution. Single-step Example Multi-step solutions are used when your solution has multiple images or solutions that break into independent steps. Avoid putting stepped diagrammars in a stepped solution, since the steppers for diagrammar and solutions are similar. If a solution has an interactive diagrammar in its first step, This is a good place for a step break to encourage engagement with the interactive. The second step contains the answer to the question. After the learner reads the last step of the solution, they lose their opportunity to answer the question. The answer should always appear on the last step of a solution so that the learner can read the earlier steps and return to answer the problem. The text on the second step doesn't directly refer to the first step. If a solution doesn't break cleanly into steps, use a single-step format, unless it makes the solution too long. Multiple-step Example

docs.text: Split-lane Mode 📏 Use `!splitlanes` when a reference image or interactive should remain on screen as the learner advances between questions. The reference image or interactive is the subject of multiple questions in this block. Because an image or interactive cannot be removed from the left lane in !splitlanes mode, the current guidance is to end the block when the visual is removed from the screen. This guarantees that, by returning to the block, the learner can still access this element after they have advanced beyond it. Example Use the left lane only if there is no single-lane solution to displaying everything learner needs to see on screen simultaneously. Minimize switching between single-lane and double-lane formats. Example: Split-lane%20Mode%2047b2086a4a774a068ba243cf717cde43/Screen_Recording_2021-06-30_at_10.56.30_PM.mov ✅ Learners should never lose access to images or interactives in the right lane. Begin a new block when you no longer need the image or interactive in `! splitlanes` mode. Blocks usually end with a major milestone within the lesson. However, in ! splitlanes mode, if you are ready to remove the object in the right lane, ending the block before arriving at the milestone is acceptable. Remember: ! splitlanes mode is only used when an interactive or image must be visible over several questions in a sequence. Example: Split-lane%20Mode%2047b2086a4a774a068ba243cf717cde43/Screen_Recording_2021-07-01_at_5.12.55_PM.mov

docs.text: Tenses 📏 We default to the present tense, except to refer to previous/forthcoming content. The Brilliant narrator uses the pronoun "we" to convey a sense of collaboration. A Brilliant lesson is an experience like no other. On this journey, the Brilliant narrator is the learner's trusted guide. We acknowledge the experience of the learner by writing in the present tense by default. We only deviate from present tense when we refer to content elsewhere in the experience, or if we are relating actual historical events. No other tense is allowed. 🚨 By the end of the lesson, we will have proved all of the double-angle formulas. ✅ In this lesson, we will prove all the double-angle formulas. We prefer the first-person plural pronoun "we" to underline the collaborative aspect of learning from a Brilliant lesson, especially when the learner inevitably makes mistakes. The narrator is a comrade, urging the learner to continue. The pronoun "you" is also allowed when "we" doesn't make exact sense. For example, "You can explore how the angle affects the outcome in the plot below." However, we avoid posing questions with a personal pronoun. Wherever possible, questions should be posed without reference to the learner or the speaker. 🚨 Can you find the length of side $A?$ ✅ What is the length of side $A?$

docs.text: Rule Blocks organize the conceptual progress of a lesson. Each block should build to one major result or concept. A block contains a complete concept or argument. At the conclusion of the block, the learner should be able to identify they have gained a new skill, tool, or concept. Accordingly, the learner has attempted several questions that support the main idea of the block. Beginning of Block The beginning of a block announces the main idea in light of the preceding content. If terminology from a previous block or lesson is needed, it is woven into the text here. All blocks begin with an informative heading. Navigating back and forth between blocks is not simple. The beginning of a block includes all information the learner needs to navigate the block successfully — even if it introduces some redundancy in the lesson. Example The first step of this block calls back to earlier content, and includes a glossary card for reference in this block. Middle of Block The middle of the block builds up an idea through a combination of questions and explanations. New terminology is introduced. Callbacks to earlier parts of the lesson are expected. There should be no question-less blocks, except for bookends. We do not use subheadings for organization within a block. Subheadings suggests your block should split into multiple blocks. End of Block The end of the block distills the main idea, highlighted by a concept box. Reinforcing questions or remarks optionally appear after the concept box. The last step of a block should extend a bridge to the next block. Example Step With Boxed Concept Step with Remark Step Looking Ahead

docs.text: Rule A step can be a single sentence or a few sentences. Steps are the basic increment of content and should advance a single, complete thought. For each step, it should be entirely clear what is being advanced. Basic example This step elaborates on a single idea: "The middle layer of this ANN selects certain input combinations." Most steps should not be longer than this one. If one of the sentences is a transitional sentence, use a line break between sentences. Example with a line break This step elaborates on a single idea: "The distance \(D\) between Buzz and the hive increases with time \(t\) ." This step includes some transitional text, which is displayed on a separate line. Transitional text may come before or after the main idea of a step. A step may contain an MCQ or lightweight question. The text in the step should introduce or include the setup information for the question. If the question is an MCQ, only the question text should appear in the solvable box. If the supporting text doesn't make sense as a complete thought, it should be displayed in the same step as the MCQ Example with question and text The supporting text above the solvable box is needed to solve the problem, and is short enough to be part of the same step as the MCQ. For questions with more supporting text, the text may be split across several steps. Try to construct these steps so that they advance single, complete thoughts leading up to the question. A step with an image can take up a lot of vertical space. Try to arrange for the entire step (image + text) to appear on the screen without requiring the learner to scroll. This may require putting the image into the right lane, or introducing the image with minimal text. Example with image and text This circle diagram image fits on screen with the descriptive text for a zoom setting near 100%. It is okay to put text above and below the image. The order of image and text should correspond to how you would like the learner to digest the step. Here, the author intended for the learner to glance at the diagram before reading the details in the second sentence. Example with image, text, and question It is possible to format a step with an MCQ, short supporting text, and an image. This is a common pattern. If the step is taller than the screen, and the question is partly off-screen upon reveal, it's not a terrible experience for the learner. Sometimes this is the only layout solution. However, a better solution is to move the MCQ to the sidekick lane. Example with image, text, and question: When you need more than two or three sentences to advance a single, complete thought, place an image or interactive a relevant image or interactive in the left lane. This layout is the only time it's acceptable to reveal more than three sentences in one step. Here, the MCQ is placed in the sidekick lane because the text, image, and MCQ are too tall for a single lane. Example with image
docs.title: Steps

docs.text: Constants, Vectors, and Matrices 📏 The class of a mathematical object should be apparent from the symbol we use to represent it. Brilliant lessons help learners develop a taste for different mathematical ideas. In our lessons, equations are not chunks of math that break up the text. Words fall into different classes and so do mathematical objects. We'd like to reflect the class of an object in the characteristics of the symbol we choose to represent it. Below are some of the naming conventions we aim to follow: Use capital letters for constants. Use lowercase, bold letters for vectors. If it's important to indicate a vector has unit length, use \hat{} . ✅ The vector $\mathbf{\hat x}-\mathbf{\hat y}$ points from $(0, 0)$ to $(1, -1).$ Use uppercase, bold letters for matrices If you have a vector/matrix, and you want to write its components, follow the convention in this example: ✅ The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is antisymmetric, so its elements obey $A_{ij} = - A_{ji}.$ Matrices are always typeset in the centered math environment using \begin{pmatrix} ... \end{pmatrix} If authoritative sources on a subject have established symbology that clashes with the guidance above, then it is more helpful to the learner if you use the more common symbol. However, we are not trapped by prevailing standards. We should weigh the advantage of unusual choices against the wider expectation. If we are intentional about the choice we're making, there's no fundamental problem with surprising notational conventions — for example, a waves course that uses \(\tau\) instead of \(2\pi.\) It is essential that deviations from the prevailing standards are flagged for the learner and supported with the reasons for deviations. We are responsible for writing content that helps learners form a mapping between our conventions and the established standards. Deviations from standards should be discussed on Slack in #editorial-lounge .

docs.text: Fractions and Rational Expressions 📏 Inline fractions are always formatted using `/` , except for simple fraction like $\frac34$or $\frac{\pi}{2}$. Guidelines for formatting fractions are similar to formatting numbers, with one complication. The built-in markup tag, \frac , formats fractions and rational expressions with a horizontal bar, which, used inline, makes expressions painful to read and wreaks havoc on line spacing within a step (go ahead and try it, then kindly delete it). Using \frac in the inline math environment is a poor reading experience for the learner. The formatting recommendation for inline fractions and rational expressions is to use the / symbol for the bar. Most fractions and simple rational expressions — like \(n/(1+n)\) — are readable formatted like this. If the content demands fractions formatted with a horizontal bar, or you need nested parentheses to write a complicated rational expression inline, then you should use \frac in the centered math environment $$$ .
docs.title: Fractions and Rational Expressions

docs.text: Functions 📏 Function notation is a pain point for learners. Nonetheless, it is used frequently across the site. Outside of a course on calculus, an advanced course that deploys techniques from calculus, or a course that is calculus-adjacent, the function notation \(f(x)\) is probably unfamiliar to a typical learner. If you use function notation, you will need to describe how to interpret it and address misconceptions (for instance, the parentheses don't mean \(f\) is multiplying \(x\) ). One of the benefits of function notation is it makes explicit how one quantity depends on another. Reminding learners of dependencies among quantities is often why we use function notation in a lesson. At this time, we have little guidance on when function notation is the correct tool in particular situations. Use your common sense. If you are using it in one or two lessons in a course, it is probably not worth the overhead cost of introducing it.

docs.text: Inline vs. Centered Math The math environment in Camperdown has two settings: inline ( $...$ ) and centered ( $$$ ). You should think of inline mode as the default for Brilliant lessons. It treats math as an extension of the text by keeping it close to the writing. The centered environment introduces a lot of white space, so it should be used judiciously. Centered environment is the correct choice in the following use cases: Multi-line calculations or derivations (should be formatted with \begin{align} ... \end{align} ) Equations or expressions that would need nested parentheses to be correct inline Equations or expressions that would be easier to read if they weren't inline To draw special attention to an equation or expression so it can be referenced easily. The tradeoff is that centered math introduces a lot of white space onto the screen, and divorces the math expressions from the text describing them, which we only want to do if necessary. If you find the need to refer to a centered mathematical expression many times, you should establish a name or symbol for it and use that for repeated inline reference.
docs.title: Inline vs. Centered Math

docs.text: Naming Variables 📏 Variables add a layer of abstraction to a Brilliant lesson. We can make this abstraction feel seamless by selecting instructive, descriptive symbols for variables. To the learner, variables should represent tangible, manipulatable objects — they shouldn't be a cypher that only a mathematical super-sleuths can decode. Because variables are things that a user manipulates in a Brilliant lesson, variables are always formatted in the math environment. When you introduce a variable in your lesson, you need to select a symbol for the variable and consider whether you should attach a descriptive subscript. Selecting a symbol To start, survey resources on the subject of the lesson. Is there a consensus on representing certain quantities by certain symbols? If so, then you should follow these conventions wherever possible. 🚨 The cruising speed of the jet, \(a,\) is faster than the speed of sound, \(D.\) 🚨 The Pythagorean Theorem, \(\zeta^2 = \eta^2 + \theta^2,\) is the relationship between the lengths of the sides of a right triangle. If there is no consensus among teaching resources, then select a symbol that doesn't collide with the variable namespace adjacent to the topic you're teaching, and that jibes with the guidance on naming constants, vectors and matrices. Attaching a subscript Next, try to give the symbol you picked a descriptive subscript — an actual English word. If the lesson has a skin, the appropriate subscript should be self-evident. Descriptive subscripts should be formatted with the \text{} markup tag. Only capitalize the subscript if it is a proper name. Sometimes, the most obvious subscript is too long. If equations involving your variables with long subscripts start looking messy, try abbreviating the subscript to the first syllable or two. Don't use a period after the abbreviation. ✅ The temperature in the house is \(T_\text{in}\) and the temperature outside is \(T_\text{out}.\) Some situations call for a variable with an index. To distinguish indices from exponents, we prefer subscript indices. Because indices are variables, they should not be formatted with the \text{} markup tag. Variables that need an index do not need a descriptive subscript. We avoid compound subscripts wherever possible. 🚨 The component of the helium molecule's velocity toward the opening is \(v_{\text{He},x}.\) The component of the nitrogen molecule's velocity in the same direction is \(v_{\text{N},x}.\) ✅ The component of the helium molecule's velocity toward the opening is \(v_x.\) The component of the nitrogen molecule's velocity in the same direction is \(w_x.\) Other situations call for variables without descriptive subscripts. If you are stating a general relationship, or if a descriptive subscript would be a burden on the learner, you can omit the subscript. ✅ A container of non-interacting gas molecules obeys the ideal gas law, \(PV = NkT.\)
docs.title: Naming Variables

docs.text: Numbers 📏 Without exception, numerical digits are displayed using the inline math environment. When displaying a number with more than three digits, place the \numcomma{} markup tag around your number for readability. Similarly, the \SI{}{} markup tag can be used to display readable numbers with units. If a number has units, the number must be displayed with \SI{}{} . Decimals less between \(-1\) and \(1\) should always have a leading \(0.\) In some cases, it might be necessary to express some words as digits, and others as words to disambiguate quantities from objects: ✅ A list with two $3$s, four $7$s, and five $1$s. Other cases where numbers should be written as words include: Numbers that begin a sentence, although this can usually be avoided by rewriting the sentence. Integers \(n\) in the range \(0 \leq n < 11\) (if they aren't used in a problem or solution). ✅ The theorems of plane geometry can be derived from five axioms postulated by Euclid in *Elements*. The numbers in addresses or years should always be formatted with the inline math environment.

docs.text: Units 📏 Most numbers in a Brilliant lesson should have units of measure attached. Calculating with unit-less numbers can yield ambiguous results. But equally important, units provide additional context to the learner. A number with units can be imagined and compared. When a number has a unit of measure, we include the unit wherever we write the number. SI units are used in Brilliant courses, universally. Unit symbols are written in lower case letters except for liter \((\text{L})\) and those units derived from the name of a person. You should never format unit symbols yourself. The built-in tag \SI{}{} will handle this for you. If you want to write just the unit symbol without a number, you can use the tag \si{} . By default, the tag \SI{}{} formats units in the denominator of a compound unit with a negative exponent — for example, \(\text{m} \ \text{s}^{-2}.\) This tag accepts an option [per-mode=symbol] that sets a / in the unit, which is more easily interpretable for most of the Brilliant audience. 👍 Near the surface of Earth, the acceleration due to gravity is roughly `$\SI[per-mode=symbol]{9.8}{\meter\per\second\squared}$.` In the above example, the option sets the unit of \(g\) as \(\text{m}/\text{s}^2\) . This option incorrect sets a compound unit with a product of units in the denominator. We are working on this capability, and this guidance will be updated when this correct setting of compound units is available. Until then, flag compound unit issues in the Slack channel #editorial-lounge . If you are writing the name of a unit in text, the names of all units start with a lower case letter except, of course, at the beginning of the sentence. There is one exception: in "degree Celsius" the unit "degree" is lower case but the modifier "Celsius" is capitalized. When a metric value is used as a modifier before a noun, hyphenating the quantity is not necessary. ✅ Can you fill a `$\SI{2}{\liter}$` bottle with this pair of pitchers?

docs.text: Rule Learners can digest lessons across the Brilliant library without encountering friction due to a different notations. A lot of mathematical notation is standard ( + will always stand for addition). It would a waste of time to make an exhaustive list of operations here. What follows is a list of operations where there's some choice in how their represented, and where Brilliant lessons fall A products of two numbers, or a scalar product of two vectors, is indicated by \cdot \(3 \cdot 4 = 12\) \(3 \times 4 = 12\) A product between a number and a variable needs no explicit operand. Multiplication is understood. Division should generally be represented by / , but if you are writing for a novice audience, the ÷ symbol is okay. Be aware that ÷ can be confused for a + on the site. The cross product between vectors is represented by \times Use \log for the natural log. If other than natural log, write the base as a subscript: \(\log_2\) Use e^{} for the exponential function, unless the expression in the exponent is exceptionally complicated, in which case use \exp() with a glossary card that describes the notation. \(\csc, \sec, \cot\) and \(\tan\) : These functions are not as familiar or as fundamental as \(\sin\) and \(\cos\) . When you use them, you are relying on a learner's ability to recall their definitions — not their reasoning or intuition. When you do use them, acknowledge that some people are not going to remember, for example, if \(\sin\) or \(\cos\) is in the numerator of \(\tan.\) Using glossary cards to provide definitions of these functions is a good solution. Inverse trig functions should be formatted like so: \(\cos^{-1}\) For other special functions, use standard symbols — \(\text{erf}, \Gamma, J_\alpha,\) and others. The symbols we use for certain operations may be slightly different for different audiences. For example, a course on partial differential equations can assume more familiarity with different notations than a course on algebra. If a course you're writing needs to deviate from the standard symbology above, raising it on Slack in #editorial-lounge is a good idea.
docs.title: Operations and Special Functions

docs.text: Abbreviations 📏 Some abbreviations are acceptable, but writing out all words completely avoids confusion. Titles in names — such as Mr. , Ms. , or Jr. — should be abbreviated. These abbreviations are common enough that writing out Mister or Junior would be awkward. Latin abbreviations are frequently found in writing, but they are not conversational. In Brilliant lessons, Latin abbreviations should be replaced by English equivalents, or in some instances, with an em dash . 🚨 Zeros of the sine function occur at integer multiples of $\pi$ (e.g., $π$, $2π$, and $3π$). ✅ Zeros of the sine function occur at integer multiples of $\pi$ — for instance $π$, $2π$, and $3\pi.$

docs.text: Acronyms Learners make associations between ideas and vocabulary through multiple exposures. Acronyms are also established through repetition. For this reason, introducing an acronym that is used in a small number of lessons is not recommended. The convention for introducing acronym However, in some courses, certain compound nouns are central to the content. These terms are repeated many times across the course, like artificial neural network in Intro to Neural Networks, and ordinary differential equation in Differential Equations I. In these instances, introducing an acronym ( ANN and ODE ) can condense text considerably, which is why we recommend it. However, it is important to establish the mapping between the term and the acronym in multiple lessons at the beginning of the course. If you are unsure about using a particular acronym, start up a conversation about it in #editorial-lounge .

docs.text: Commas 📏 Put a comma everywhere it is required, but only place optional commas when they enhance the clarity of a sentence. Some commas are required. For example: ✅ If a function's derivative is strictly nonzero, then it is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. However, other commas are a matter of taste: ✅ After relaxing the constraint, the number of microstates accessible to the system does not decrease. Placing a comma everywhere you are allowed to place a comma leads to writing that is over-punctuated, so which optional commas should you keep? Broadly, commas help learner understand the structure of a sentence. If you're not sure whether to include an optional comma in your writing, you may want to consider these two questions: Does omitting a comma make the sentence ambiguous, or harder to parse at a glance? When you read the sentence aloud, is there a short, natural pause where you would place the comma? When you read the sentence aloud, is there a short, natural pause where you would place the comma? If the answer to either question is yes, then you should include the comma. 📏 Put a comma everywhere it is required, but only place optional commas when they enhance the clarity of a sentence. Some commas are required. For example: ✅ If a function's derivative is strictly nonzero, then it is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing. However, other commas are a matter of taste: ✅ After relaxing the constraint, the number of microstates accessible to the system does not decrease. Placing a comma everywhere you are allowed to place a comma leads to writing that is over-punctuated, so which optional commas should you keep? docs.text: Ellipses 📏 Ellipses on Brilliant usually mean `and so on`, and are valid end punctuation. Ellipses indicate an omitted word or phrase from a sentence, but this usage is rare in Brilliant courses outside of character dialogues. An ellipsis is also shorthand for and so on in a list. This usage is fine in the math setting by way of the built-in command \dots ✅ The centers of the blocks in the tower follow a harmonic progression: $1/2$ , $1/4$, $1/6$, $1/8$, $\dots$ Note that there is no period following the \dots . It is clear that the sentence ends after the list. Including an extra period after the ellipsis does not provide additional clarity, so it is recommended to omit it. For the sake of clarity, refrain from using an ellipsis in a list of words. It is better in this situation to use phrases like and so on , and more , or and others . ✅ Along the way, we'll encounter spacetimes with different properties — Ernst spacetime, Friedman-Robertson-Walker, Bertotti-Kasner, and others. Ellipses are occasionally used to convey anticipation or uncertainty. For example, 🚨 We've seen that the derivative of an object's position is its velocity, but other situations where derivatives arise naturally are on the horizon... The Brilliant narrator is a trusted, confident voice that promotes learning, so we recommend against this usage outside of character dialogues.
docs.title: Ellipses

docs.text: Em dashes 📏 Whenever you want to use a colon, semicolon, or parenthesis — opt for the em dash. The em dash is a versatile punctuation mark that can stand in for the colon, semicolon, parentheses, and even the comma, as a bolder, more visual alternative. As with the marks it replaces, it always appears mid-sentence where you would normally find another mark of punctuation. (to make one, use --- in Camperdown. Do NOT use your operating systems' built in command). 📏 To single out a point, use an em dash. If minus signs are nearby, then use a colon. 📏 To set off parenthetical information, use two em dashes. Semicolons are ... dead, except in academic writing. For this reason, they are not used in Brilliant courses. Whenever you are inclined to use a semicolon — and we sincerely hope you never are — we recommend using an em dash instead. 📏 To create an emphatic pause between two clauses, use an em dash. If minus signs are nearby, then use a period to separate them into individual sentences. What about the comma, you ask? Replacing a comma with an em dash in a sentence is not straightforward to determine. We suggest two rules of thumb: If the sentence contains multiple phrases or clauses, try using an em dash to reveal its structure at a glance. If you would add a longer pause (for comprehension or for dramatic effect) while saying the sentence, then the longer pause should be indicated by an em dash. If minus signs are in close proximity (~in the same grouping of sentences), then do not use an em dash. In this case, use the comma or separate your clauses into consecutive sentences. Examples Singling out a point 🚨 And here's the point: punctuation is old fashioned. ✅ And here's the point — punctuation is old fashioned. Pause between clauses 🚨 The dot product of two vectors is not a vector, but a scalar; it is a number that is the same in all coordinate systems. ✅ The dot product of two vectors is not a vector, but a scalar — it is a number that is the same in all coordinate systems. 🚨 Any jackass can kick down a barn; it takes a good carpenter to build one. ✅ Any jackass can kick down a barn, but it takes a good carpenter to build one. ✅ Any jackass can kick down a barn. It takes a good carpenter to build one. Replacing enclosing commas or parenthesis ✅ If the planets went in circles — as they nearly do — the time required to go around the circle would be proportional to the 3/2 power of the diameter.

docs.text: Italic 📏 The only permitted use of italics is to denote the titles of books, movies, or other published materials. Italicized terms are not easy to distinguish from normal text — they "hide out" until you arrive at them. The only use of italics prescribed for Brilliant lessons is to designate titles of books, movies, or other published materials. You can convey conversational importance on a word by italicizing it, but understand that it is likely to be missed on a skim. If it is crucial that people notice your emphasis, always use bold. 🚨 "Jack seemed happy, he *really* liked that birdwatching shirt you got him!" 🚨 Which algorithm would *not* finish in polynomial time? ✅ Which algorithm would **not** **finish in polynomial time?

docs.text: Lists 📏 The serial or Oxford comma is mandatory in a list of items. When you are listing items, the final comma before the conjunction is optional. However, on Brilliant it is not. This comma announces the last item of the series visually, so we include it to ease scanning of the lesson text. 🚨 An ellipse is drawn using two tacks, a loop of string and a pencil. ✅ An ellipse is drawn using two tacks, a loop of string, and a pencil.

docs.text: Parentheses 📏 Parentheses can be used when an em dash won't suffice. Parentheses are overloaded symbols in mathematical writing. Although their meaning is usually clear from context, they are primarily used in Brilliant lessons as mathematical symbols. ✅ One particular parametric representation of the unit circle is $(\cos t, \sin t).$ In text, it is recommended to replace parentheses with em dashes. However, there are certain situations where using parentheses is unavoidable. For example, if other punctuation is necessary after the end of the parenthetical phrase: ✅ Under this modification, if action and reaction are still equal (and they must be in the long run), momentum will be conserved as before.

docs.text: Quotes 📏 Quotes are used to designate a short, inline quotation, or to coin a term. A block quote (initiated by the ! quote command in Camperdown) is the preferred way to format a quote. However, using a block for a short quote — less than a sentence or two — is awkward. In Brilliant lessons, quotation marks are used for inline formatting of short quotes. In rare instances, quotes may be used when you are coining a term. ✅ Your invention, which we will call a "Decision Box," decides what move to play based on what was played on the previous turn.

docs.text: Titles 📏 The first letter of all major words in lesson titles and block headings should be capitalized. The first letter of a minor word like `a`, `an`, `the`, `to`, `or`, `and`, or `with` should not be capitalized, unless it is the first or last word in the title or heading. This formatting guideline is identical to the so-called "headline" style used for titles of most publications. Specifically, the first letters of articles, conjunctions and prepositions — regardless of length — are not capitalized. All other words should begin with a capital letter. 🚨 `! heading1[Calculus is the language of science]` ✅ `! heading1[Calculus is the Language of Science]` On the front bookend, the lesson title is displayed by default as a large heading — the same style as the subsequent block headings. For consistency, the lesson title and block headings should have the same case. Wherever possible, avoid using the inline math environment in a block heading. The math formatting is ugly beside the large, boldface heading text. Since we write about STEM topics, avoidance is going to be an inadequate solution sometimes. If you want to use math formatting in a heading, please flag it in the #editorial-lounge slack channel.
docs.title: Titles

docs.text: Rule Boldface is our primary means for emphasizing single terms in a Brilliant lesson. The main use of boldface is to highlight the course-specific vocabulary needed to comprehend the lesson. Boldface text stands out from normal text. You can easily pick out boldface terms as you scroll through a block. The first instance of a new piece of vocabulary should always be set in boldface, regardless of whether it is a glossary term . Example The combination of a car's speed and direction is called its velocity , represented in the figure by a yellow arrow. Boldface can also be used to attract attention to essential words. In selecting words to bold, consider words would be easy to misread or gloss over otherwise. If the learner misses these words, they probably miss the question. Example How many people do you expect will have a positive test result and do not have the disease? An entire phrase in boldface is meaningless. Single terms — no more than a few words — are the largest unit of content to appear in boldface.
docs.title: Boldface

docs.text: Rule Use the inline code environment for individual commands and snippets that are less than a line of code. Otherwise, use a code block. Like numbers and variables, code and pseudocode should not appear as plain text in a Brilliant lesson. In Camperdown, there is a specific code display environment that is initiated by the escape `...`. This inline code environment is used for single commands up to a full line of code from a program. If a code snippet is longer than a line in a program, or if the line is long enough that it breaks across lines in the lesson, the escape ``` initiates a code block. When you include a block of code that is not pseudocode, write the name of the language in which it's written in the first line.
docs.title: Code

docs.text: Rule Glossary cards display the meanings of important terms. They are placed so that users don't need to navigate to an earlier block or lesson to look up the meaning of a term. Don't use a glossary card on the first use of a term in a course. The term should be formatted in boldface, and may appear in a concept box. When a term appears next, after the block in which it is first introduced, attach a glossary card to it with the Camperdown markup [term](glossary "label for term") The same glossary card should not appear more than once in the same block — learners can scroll up to find glossary cards. Too many glossary cards in a lesson feels clutter-y. Assume the learner needs to reference the glossary card in the first ~3 blocks in which the term is used. When a term is used in a subsequent lesson, the first occurrence should have a glossary card attached. You don't need to attach a card to subsequent occurrences in the same lesson. If a term is central to the course, and is used in nearly every lesson after it is introduced, stop attaching a glossary card to the first occurrence of the term after the third lesson after it was introduced.
docs.title: Glossary Cards

docs.text: Rule Learners can digest lessons across the Brilliant library without encountering friction due to a different notations. A lot of mathematical notation is standard ( + will always stand for addition). It would a waste of time to make an exhaustive list of operations here. What follows is a list of operations where there's some choice in how their represented, and where Brilliant lessons fall A products of two numbers, or a scalar product of two vectors, is indicated by \cdot \(3 \cdot 4 = 12\) \(3 \times 4 = 12\) A product between a number and a variable needs no explicit operand. Multiplication is understood. Division should generally be represented by / , but if you are writing for a novice audience, the ÷ symbol is okay. Be aware that ÷ can be confused for a + on the site. The cross product between vectors is represented by \times Use \log for the natural log. If other than natural log, write the base as a subscript: \(\log_2\) Use e^{} for the exponential function, unless the expression in the exponent is exceptionally complicated, in which case use \exp() with a glossary card that describes the notation. \(\csc, \sec, \cot\) and \(\tan\) : These functions are not as familiar or as fundamental as \(\sin\) and \(\cos\) . When you use them, you are relying on a learner's ability to recall their definitions — not their reasoning or intuition. When you do use them, acknowledge that some people are not going to remember, for example, if \(\sin\) or \(\cos\) is in the numerator of \(\tan.\) Using glossary cards to provide definitions of these functions is a good solution. Inverse trig functions should be formatted like so: \(\cos^{-1}\) For other special functions, use standard symbols — \(\text{erf}, \Gamma, J_\alpha,\) and others. The symbols we use for certain operations may be slightly different for different audiences. For example, a course on partial differential equations can assume more familiarity with different notations than a course on algebra. If a course you're writing needs to deviate from the standard symbology above, raising it on Slack in #editorial-lounge is a good idea.
docs.title: Operations and Special Functions

docs.text: In some Brilliant lessons, we attach technical importance to strings of letters that are not necessarily words. Working with these objects is central to the learning experience, so they are formatted in the math environment. For example, in the computational biology course, nucleotide sequences are given to and manipulated by the learner to solve problems. Example The "TATA box" is a canonical example — a short nucleotide sequence \(\tt{TATAAAA}\) found on the coding strand just before the start of the gene. In a logic course, the objects a learner manipulates could be logic gates: Example The simplest half-adder design incorporates \(\mathbf{XOR}\) and \(\mathbf{AND}.\) Similar to the guidance for equations, strings are punctuated as if they were nouns in a sentence, and the punctuation is typeset in the math environment to avoid hanging punctuation. In a lesson you're writing, if the learner will manipulate objects represented by strings, math formatting is probably correct, but because these cases are rare, we encourage you to post about your use case in the Slack channel #editorial-lounge .
